text: This video shows a laparoscopic repair of a morgani hernia. A thirty-two years old male patient presents with two month history of dyspnea and abdominal discomfort. The patient has no previous history of abdominal operations or major trauma. CT scan evaluation revealed a large morgani hernia containing omenal fat.

The surgeon uses a three-port laparoscopic technique to explore the abdomen, revealing an anterior diaphragmatic defect with herniation of the greater omentum. Grasping forceps are used to pull the omentum down and reposition it into the abdominal cavity. The hernia sac is dissected and excised from the thoracic region.

Many technical aspects are still under debate. Excision of the sac is suggested in order to reduce recurrence rate if it can be dissected away from the mediastinal structures easily. The advantage of excising the hernia sac is to avoid a potentially empty space left behind in the thoracic cavity. Life-threatening complications, massive pneumomediastinum and damage to the pericardium or mediastinal structures were reported during the dissection of the sac.
Following the excision, the dimensions of the diaphragmatic defect are visible. The surgeon removes the excised hernia sac from the thoracic cavity using an endobag.
The surgeon repairs the diaphragmatic defect using interrupted non-absorbable sutures, including X-shaped stitches and U-shaped mattress sutures, to ensure closure and minimize recurrence risk.

The surgeon performs a mesh repair technique for the hernia using a tailored composite polypropylene mesh, which is fixed in place with absorbable fixation devices. The mesh is positioned to reinforce the closure and reduce tension, aiming to decrease the recurrence rate.

No drains were placed after the procedure. The patient made an eventful recovery and was discharged on the fifth postoperative day. He was symptom free at five years follow up. The control CT scan showed no hernia recurrence.
